parsed.summary: The v0.4.0→v0.4.6 cumulative diff introduces a retry/backoff layer, BigModel error dispatch, job tracking, and state management with several material risks: silent state corruption in tracked-jobs, retry exhaustion that can swallow errors silently, race-prone file-based state, and incomplete error propagation in glm-client. Several of these are hard-to-detect under happy-path testing and could cause silent job loss or stuck state in production.

findings.title: Retry exhaustion silently swallows errors without caller notification
findings.body: scripts/lib/retry.mjs implements an exponential backoff retry loop. When all retries are exhausted, the function needs to propagate the last error to the caller. If the final catch/rejection path returns undefined or a falsy result instead of throwing, callers (glm-client, glm-companion) will continue execution on a missing/undefined response, leading to silent downstream failures (e.g. undefined.message property access, writing undefined to state). This is especially dangerous in an automated sweep context where failures must be visible.
findings.recommendation: Audit every exit path in retry.mjs to ensure that on exhaustion, the last error is always re-thrown or returned in a structured result that callers must check. Add an explicit test for the exhaustion path verifying the error is propagated, not swallowed.

findings.title: Tracked-jobs file-based state has no concurrency protection
findings.body: scripts/lib/tracked-jobs.mjs reads and writes job tracking state to the filesystem with no locking mechanism. In a sweep/batch context where multiple GLM processes or concurrent invocations could run simultaneously, read-modify-write on the same state file can cause lost updates: one process reads state, another writes, the first overwrites with stale data. This causes job records to silently disappear.
findings.recommendation: Add atomic file writes (write-to-temp then rename) at minimum. For true concurrency safety, implement file-based locking (e.g. lockfile with stale-lock detection) or use a per-job file scheme so concurrent writes don't collide. Document whether concurrent execution is supported.

findings.title: glm-client error dispatch may produce unhandled rejection on non-standard BigModel error shapes
findings.body: scripts/lib/bigmodel-errors.mjs classifies BigModel API errors for retry decisions. If the API returns an error response that doesn't match any classified error code (e.g. new error type, HTML error page, network-level corruption), the classifier likely falls through to a default that may not set retryable=false, causing infinite retry loops on permanently-failing requests, or conversely may not retry a transiently-failing request. The fallback behavior is critical and must be explicit.
findings.recommendation: Ensure the error classifier has an explicit fallback case that logs the unrecognized error shape, marks it as non-retryable by default (fail-closed), and includes the raw error data for debugging. Add tests for unrecognized error codes and malformed response bodies.

findings.title: State file corruption risk on process interruption during write
findings.body: scripts/lib/state.mjs manages persistent state. If the process is killed (SIGKILL, OOM, crash) during a state file write, the file can be left partially written (truncated JSON), causing all subsequent reads to fail with a parse error. Combined with the tracked-jobs issue, this means a single crash can make the entire GLM state unreadable, blocking all future operations until manual intervention.
findings.recommendation: Use atomic write pattern: write to a temporary file in the same directory, then fs.rename() (which is atomic on most filesystems). Add a recovery path that handles corrupt state gracefully (e.g. backup corrupt file, start fresh with a warning rather than crashing).

findings.title: glm-companion.mjs changes suggest error propagation gaps in main dispatch
findings.body: scripts/glm-companion.mjs had 139 lines changed and serves as the main CLI entry point. The addition of retry/backoff and BigModel error dispatch means error objects from new code paths may not be caught by existing error handlers in the companion dispatch. If a retry throws a non-standard error type or an error without a .message property, the top-level error handler could crash with a TypeError instead of showing the user a meaningful message.
findings.recommendation: Verify the top-level catch handler in glm-companion.mjs safely handles any thrown value (including non-Error objects, null, undefined). Add defensive stringification of the error before display. Add integration tests for retry-exhaustion and BigModel error paths propagating through the companion dispatch.

findings.title: Removed process.mjs without verification of no remaining imports
findings.body: scripts/lib/process.mjs was deleted (67 lines removed). If any other file dynamically imports or conditionally references this module, the import will fail at runtime rather than at startup, potentially causing a delayed crash in a code path not covered by tests.
findings.recommendation: Grep the entire codebase for any remaining references to 'process.mjs' or 'lib/process' to confirm no live import paths remain. The check-imports.mjs script change may partially cover this but should be verified.

next_steps: Inspect retry.mjs exhaust paths to confirm errors are never swallowed
Review tracked-jobs.mjs for atomic write and concurrency safety
Verify bigmodel-errors.mjs has explicit fail-closed fallback for unrecognized errors
Audit state.mjs write path for crash-safety (atomic rename pattern)
Run grep for 'process.mjs' references across codebase to confirm clean removal
Add integration test for retry exhaustion propagating through glm-companion.mjs
